perturbation.original_text: 1.70 "Licensed Product" means any pharmaceutical product, including all forms, presentations, strengths, doses and formulations (including any method of delivery), that contains leronlimab (PRO-140) as defined by [***] that CytoDyn is currently evaluating in its clinical development program for the treatment of HIV infection that is Covered by one or more claims of a CytoDyn Patent or CytoDyn Know-How.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.70 "Licensed Product" means any product that Vyera chooses to commercialize under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Licensed Product' is being removed, which introduces uncertainty about which products are actually covered under the licensing agreement. By removing the original definition, the changed text creates an ambiguity with respect to which products Vyera is authorized to commercialize. This impacts Section 2.1(a), where CytoDyn grants Vyera an exclusive license under CytoDyn Patents and Know-How to commercialize the 'Licensed Products', creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 Grant of Licenses. (a) License to Vyera. CytoDyn hereby grants to Vyera, and Vyera hereby accepts, an exclusive royalty-bearing license (or sublicense, as the case may be), under the CytoDyn Patents, the CytoDyn Know-How and the Inventions (if any) solely to Commercialize, use, have used, offer for sale and sell Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by redefining "Licensed Product" to be any product Vyera chooses to commercialize, introduces a contradiction with Section 2.1(a). Section 2.1(a) grants Vyera an exclusive license to commercialize 'Licensed Products' under CytoDyn Patents and Know-How. The original definition limited 'Licensed Product' to those containing leronlimab (PRO-140) and covered by CytoDyn Patents or Know-How. The new definition removes this constraint, potentially allowing Vyera to commercialize any product, even those unrelated to CytoDyn's Patents or Know-How, under the guise of the license granted in 2.1(a). This introduces ambiguity about the scope of the license, directly contradicting the original intent and creating a significant inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: 1.26 "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" means: (a) with respect to the efforts to be expended, or considerations to be undertaken, by a Party or its Affiliate with respect to any objective, activity or decision to be undertaken hereunder, reasonable, good faith efforts to accomplish such objective, activity or decision as such Party would normally use to accomplish a similar objective, activity or decision under similar circumstances; and (b) with respect to Development and Commercialization of any Licensed Product for any indication by a Party, efforts and resources consistent with those efforts and resources commonly used by a similarly situated biotechnology company with respect to a product owned by it or to which it has similar rights, which product is at a similar stage in its development or product life and is of similar market potential taking into account (i) the patent and other proprietary position of the Licensed Product and (ii) the anticipated profitability of the Licensed Product.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.26 "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" means whatever Vyera determines is best for its business.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts' has been omitted and replaced with a much more lenient definition, which means that Vyera would have more flexibility in making decisions. Section 4.1, states that "CytoDyn shall use Commercially Reasonable Efforts to execute and perform the activities set forth in the Development Plan", which creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 Development Plan and Development Activities. CytoDyn shall have sole responsibility for, and final decision-making authority with respect to, performance of Development of the Licensed Product for the Initial Indication and any Subsequent Indication. CytoDyn shall use Commercially Reasonable Efforts to execute and perform the activities set forth in the Development Plan. CytoDyn shall conduct the activities under the Development Plan, and shall ensure that its Affiliates and contractors conduct their activities under the Development Plan, in a good scientific manner and in material compliance with Applicable Law, including cGLP, cGCP, cGMP and applicable national and international guidelines. For clarity, the Development Plan will only include activities related to indications in the Field.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts' with specific criteria related to industry standards and profitability. The changed text replaces this with 'whatever Vyera determines is best for its business,' giving Vyera broad discretion. Section 4.1 requires CytoDyn to use 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts.' The original definition provided a benchmark for CytoDyn's efforts, which the changed definition removes, creating a contradiction. Now, Vyera's subjective determination dictates the level of effort required of CytoDyn, conflicting with the previous objective standard. This is a strong contradiction because it fundamentally alters CytoDyn's obligation and creates uncertainty about the enforceability of Section 4.1.

perturbation.original_text: 5.5 Decisions that are not Reserved. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Article 5 or any other section of this Agreement: (a) the Minimum Requirements may not be modified, amended or otherwise changed without the written consent of CytoDyn, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed; and [***].
perturbation.changed_text: 5.5 Decisions that are not Reserved. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Article 5 or any other section of this Agreement: [***].
perturbation.explanation: Clause (a) is being removed from 5.5. It creates an ambiguity and in-text contradiction about how decisions that are not reserved should be handled. Section 5.2 states, "Vyera shall conduct the Commercialization activities in accordance with the Commercialization Plan and in performing such activities will ensure that it meets or exceeds the Minimum Requirements". With the change, it makes unclear, how Vyera can fail to meet the Minimum requirements without breaking the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2 Commercialization Plan. Attached as Attachment C is a written commercialization plan setting forth anticipated material Commercialization activities to be performed for the Licensed Product in the Initial Indication in the Territory (the "Commercialization Plan"). Vyera shall conduct the Commercialization activities in accordance with the Commercialization Plan and in performing such activities will ensure that it meets or exceeds the Minimum Requirements. No later than three (3) months prior to the anticipated First Commercial Sale in the Territory based upon the then most recent Development Plan, Vyera shall update the Commercialization Plan, and shall thereafter update the Commercialization Plan on an annual basis by providing the JC with such updates no later than November 1 of each Calendar Year. In each case, the Commercialization Plan shall, at a minimum, include the activities, investments and allocations set forth in the Minimum Requirements. To the extent that CytoDyn files any BLA with a Regulatory Authority to cover a Subsequent Indication in the Field not included within the then current Licensed Product target label and the FDA accepts such BLA filing for review on or before September 1 of any Calendar Year, the updated Commercialization Plan shall include the Commercialization activities, if any, to be performed with respect to the Licensed Product in such Subsequent Indication.
perturbation.justification: The original text of section 5.5 provided a specific condition (Clause a) that the Minimum Requirements could not be modified without CytoDyn's written consent. Section 5.2 states that Vyera must meet or exceed the Minimum Requirements. By removing clause (a) from section 5.5, it creates a contradiction. Without clause (a), it becomes ambiguous how the Minimum Requirements are handled. Section 5.2 clearly dictates Vyera's obligation to meet the Minimum Requirements. Therefore, the removal creates a direct contradiction and a significant ambiguity, making enforcement of Section 5.2 unclear.
